name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُل لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يَغْفِرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ أَيَّامَ ٱللَّهِ لِيَجْزِىَ قَوْمًۢا بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
Say to the faithful to forgive those who do not expect Allah’s days, that He may [Himself] requite every people for what they used to earn. (14)
```

```arabic
مَنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ أَسَآءَ فَعَلَيْهَا ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ (١٥)
```

```english
Whoever acts righteously, it is for his own soul, and whoever does evil, it is to its own detriment, then you will be brought back to your Lord. (15)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَفَضَّلْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦)
```

```english
Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood and We provided them with all the good things. We gave them an advantage over all the nations, (16)
```

```arabic
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم بَيِّنَـٰتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ ۖ فَمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوٓا۟ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (١٧)
```

```english
and We gave them manifest precepts. But they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ. (17)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَىٰ شَرِيعَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ فَٱتَّبِعْهَا وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٨)
```

```english
Then We set you upon a clear course of the Law; so follow it, and do not follow the desires of those who do not know. (18)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُمْ لَن يُغْنُوا۟ عَنكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٩)
```

```english
Indeed they will not avail you in any way against Allah. Indeed the wrongdoers are allies of one another, but Allah is the ally of the Godwary. (19)
```

## (Commentary)

After mentioning the verses of divine unity (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and alluding therein to the Resurrection (المعاد) and Prophethood (النبوة) within the context of the revelation of the Book and the warning to the arrogant who mock it, He mentions in these verses the legislation of the Law (الشريعة) for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. He approaches this through two premises that link it to the preceding discourse. The first is the call to the believers to refrain from confronting the disbelievers (الكفار) who do not expect the "Days of Allah" (أيام الله), for Allah will requite them, as all deeds—whether righteous or evil—are subject to accountability. This is the reason for the legislation of the Law. The second premise is that the sending down of the Book, Authority (الحكمة), and Prophethood is not an unprecedented innovation (بدع); indeed, Allah gave the Children of Israel the Book, Authority, and Prophethood, and granted them clear proofs (البينات) such that no doubt could remain for any doubter regarding the religion of Allah, except that their scholars differed therein out of envy (بغيا) among themselves, and Allah will judge between them.

Then, the Glorified mentions the legislation of the Law for him, commanding him to follow it and forbidding him from following the whims (أهواء) of the ignorant.

His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يَغْفِرُوا لِلَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ أَيَّامَ اللهِ** **"Say to those who have faith that they should forgive those who do not expect the days of Allah"** (45:14) etc. This is a command from the Exalted to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to command the believers to forgive the disbelievers. Thus, the implicit structure of the verse is: "Say to them: Forgive, and they shall forgive." This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ لِعِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يُقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ** **"Say to My servants who have faith that they should maintain the prayer"** (14:31).

The verse is Meccan, situated within the context of the preceding verses describing the state of the arrogant who mock the signs of Allah and threatening them with the severest punishment. It appears that when the believers in the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saw these mockers persisting in their slander and insults against the Prophet and their mockery of Allah's signs, they could not restrain themselves from defending the Book of Allah and the one sent with it, and from calling them to renounce their ways and believe—even though they were among those against whom the word of punishment had become due, as is apparent from the previous verses. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to order them to pardon and overlook them, and not to concern themselves with their state, for the evil consequences of their deeds will surely overtake them, and the recompense for what they have earned will reach them.

Accordingly, the meaning of "forgiveness" (المغفرة) in His saying: **قُلْ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يَغْفِرُوا** "Say to those who have faith that they should forgive" is to overlook and turn away from them by ceasing to quarrel or argue with them. The meaning of **الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ أَيَّامَ اللهِ** "those who do not expect the days of Allah" refers to those mentioned in the previous verses; they do not anticipate any "Days of Allah" wherein no judgment or sovereignty belongs to anyone but Allah, the Exalted—such as the day of death, the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection*, the Day of Resurrection, and the day of exterminating punishment.

His saying: **لِيَجْزِيَ قَوْماً بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ** **"so that He may [one day] reward a people for what they used to earn"** (45:14) is the rationale for the command to forgive, or for the command to issue the command to forgive. The gist is: let them overlook them and not confront them, for there is no need for that, as Allah will requite them for what they used to earn. Thus, the verse is similar to His saying: **وَذَرْنِي وَالْمُكَذِّبِينَ أُولِي النَّعْمَةِ وَمَهِّلْهُمْ قَلِيلاً إِنَّ لَدَيْنا أَنْكالاً وَجَحِيماً** **"Leave Me to deal with the deniers, the possessors of ease and plenty, and give them a little respite. Indeed with Us are heavy fetters and a fierce fire"** (73:11-12), and His saying: **ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِي خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ** **"then leave them to play around in their impious gossip"** (6:91), and His saying: **فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا وَيَلْعَبُوا حَتَّى يُلاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ** **"So leave them to gossip and play until they encounter their day which they are promised"** (70:42), and His saying: **فَاصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَقُلْ سَلامٌ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ** **"So pardon them and say, ‘Peace!’ Soon they will know"** (43:89).

The meaning of the verse is: Command those who believe to pardon and overlook those arrogant ones who mock the signs of Allah and do not anticipate the Days of Allah, so that Allah may requite them for what they used to earn—and the Day of Recompense is one of His Days. That is, let them overlook these deniers of the Days of Allah until He requites them for their deeds on one of His Days.

In His saying: **لِيَجْزِيَ قَوْماً** "so that He may reward a people," a manifest noun is used in place of a pronoun. The apparent requirement was to say: "so that He may reward them." The subtle point (النكتة) in this—along with the word "people" (قوماً) being an indefinite noun without an adjective—is to belittle their matter and show a lack of concern for them, as if they are an unknown group whose identity is unrecognized and whose affairs are of no importance.

Through the aforementioned explanation of the verse's meaning, this verse and what follows it connect to what preceded it, and the objections raised against it—which others have struggled to answer—are dispelled. Furthermore, the corruption of the various meanings they have mentioned for it becomes clear; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the more extensive commentaries.

His saying, the Exalted: **مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ أَساءَ فَعَلَيْها ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ** **"Whoever acts righteously, it is for his own soul, and whoever does evil, it is to its own detriment; then you will be brought back to your Lord"** (45:15). This stands as the rationale for His saying: **لِيَجْزِيَ قَوْماً** "so that He may reward a people" etc., which is why it is not joined by a conjunction and is not a new beginning (استئناف) at all.

The sum of the meaning is: Allah will requite them for what they earned, for deeds do not vanish in vain or without effect; rather, whoever performs a righteous deed (العمل الصالح) benefits from it, and whoever does an evil deed is harmed by it. Then to your Lord you shall return, and He will requite you according to your deeds: if good, then good; and if evil, then evil.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ** **"Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood"** (45:16) etc. Having explained that deeds have good or bad effects that attach to their doers, He intended to draw attention to the legislation of a Law for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, as it is incumbent upon Allah, the Glorified, to guide His servants to that which contains their goodness and felicity, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ وَمِنْها جائِرٌ** **"With Allah rests the guidance to the straight path, and some of them are devious"** (16:9).

He drew attention to this in His upcoming saying: **ثُمَّ جَعَلْناكَ عَلى شَرِيعَةٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ** **"Then We set you on a course of the Law"** (45:18) etc. He preceded this by referring to what He gave the Children of Israel of the Book, Authority, and Prophethood, and His providing them with good things, His favoring them, and His giving them clear proofs. This was to announce that the divine outpouring (الإفاضة) of the Law, Prophethood, and the Book is not an unprecedented innovation without prior example; rather, it has a parallel in the Children of Israel, who are within their sight and hearing.

So, in His saying: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ**, the "Book" (الكتاب) refers to the Torah, which contains the Law of Moses عليه‌السلام. As for the Gospel (الإنجيل), it does not contain a Law; its Law is the Law of the Torah. As for the Psalms (زبور) of David, they consist of supplications and litanies. It is possible that "the Book" refers to the genus that includes the Torah, the Gospel, and the Psalms, as has been suggested, but this is made unlikely by the fact that "the Book" is not used in the Qur'an except for that which contains a Law.

The "Authority" (الحكمة), by the context of its mention alongside the Book, refers to that which the Book judges and decrees regarding the duties of the people, as mentioned in His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ** **"and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, so that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed"** (2:213). He said regarding the Torah: **يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ الَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا لِلَّذِينَ هادُوا وَالرَّبَّانِيُّونَ وَالْأَحْبارُ بِمَا اسْتُحْفِظُوا مِنْ كِتابِ اللهِ** **"by which the prophets who had submitted [to Allah] would judge for the Jews, and [so would] the priests and the scribes, as they were entrusted with the Book of Allah"** (5:44). Thus, Authority is an inseparable concomitant of the Book, just as Prophethood is an inseparable concomitant of it.

The meaning of "Prophethood" (النبوة) is well-known; Allah raised a vast multitude of prophets from the Children of Israel, as stated in the narrations, and He recounted a group of their messengers in His Book.

His saying: **وَرَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ** **"and We provided them with all the good things"** (45:16) means wholesome provisions, among which were manna and quails.

His saying: **وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ** **"and We gave them an advantage over all the nations"** (45:16). If the meaning is all the worlds (العالمين) — *all creation across all times*, then they were favored in certain respects, such as the abundance of prophets sent to them and the many manifest miracles performed by their prophets. If the meaning is the people of their own time, then they were favored in all respects.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَآتَيْناهُمْ بَيِّناتٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ** **"and We gave them manifest proofs of the Law"** (45:17) to the end of the verse. The "clear proofs" (البينات) refer to the manifest signs that remove every doubt and uncertainty and erase it from the truth. This is evidenced by the subsequent clause: **فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ** **"and they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them"**.

Regarding "the Law" (الأمر), it is said to be the matter of religion (الدين), and the preposition "from" (من) means "in." The meaning would be: "We gave them clear proofs in the matter of religion," which includes the miracles of Moses عليه‌السلام.

It is also said that it refers to the matter of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The meaning would be: "We gave them signs regarding the matter of the Prophet and clear marks of his truthfulness," such as his appearance in Mecca, his migration from there to Yathrib, the support of his people, and other things mentioned in their books.

His saying: **فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ** **"and they did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves"** (45:17) indicates that the differences that appeared among them in religion and the mixing of falsehood with truth did not arise from ambiguity or ignorance; rather, their scholars created them out of envy, and envy was prevalent among them.

His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ** **"Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ"** (45:17) is an indication that their differing—which is not free from the mixing of falsehood with truth—does not vanish in vain; it will surely have its effect, and Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection, requiting them according to what their deeds necessitate.

His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ جَعَلْناكَ عَلى شَرِيعَةٍ مِنَ الْأَمْرِ فَاتَّبِعْها وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** **"Then We set you on a course of the Law; so follow it, and do not follow the desires of those who do not know"** (45:18). The address is to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and his community (الأمة) shares in it. The "Law" (الشريعة) literally means a path leading to water, and "the matter" (الأمر) refers to the matter of religion. The meaning is: after We gave the Children of Israel what We gave them, We set you upon a specific path of the divine religion, which is the Islamic Law that Allah has specifically assigned to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and his community.

His saying: **فَاتَّبِعْها** "so follow it" etc., is a command to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to follow what is revealed to him of the religion and not to follow the whims of the ignorant that oppose the divine religion.

From the verse, it appears, firstly: that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is legally obligated (مكلف) by the religion just like the rest of the community.

Secondly: that every practical ruling (حكم عملي) that is not based on divine revelation (الوحي) and does not originate from it is merely one of the whims of the ignorant, unrelated to knowledge (العلم).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّهُمْ لَنْ يُغْنُوا عَنْكَ مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً** **"Indeed they will not avail you in any way against Allah"** (45:19) etc. This is the rationale for the prohibition against following the whims of those who do not know. "Availing" (الإغناء) against something means removing the need for it. The gist is: you have essential needs before Allah, the Glorified, which none but He can fulfill, and the means to that is following His religion and nothing else. Therefore, these people whose whims you might follow cannot fulfill any of your needs for you, nor can they provide any degree of availment.

His saying: **وَإِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ وَاللهُ وَلِيُّ الْمُتَّقِينَ** **"Indeed the wrongdoers are allies of one another, but Allah is the ally of the God-conscious"** (45:19). What the context provides is that this is another rationale for the prohibition against following the whims of the ignorant. The "wrongdoers" (الظالمين) — *the unjust who overstep the bounds of truth* — are those who follow their invented whims, and the "God-conscious" (المتقين) — *those who possess godwariness (تقوى) and protect themselves from Allah's displeasure* — are those who follow the religion of Allah.

The meaning is: Allah is the Guardian (الولي) of those who follow His religion because they are God-conscious, and Allah is their Guardian. As for those who follow the whims of the ignorant, He, the Exalted, is not their Guardian; rather, they are guardians of one another because they are wrongdoers, and the wrongdoers are guardians of one another. Therefore, follow the religion of Allah so that He may be your Guardian, and do not follow their whims, lest they become your guardians, for they will not avail you in any way against Allah.

Naming those who follow other than the religion of Allah as "wrongdoers" (الظالمين) is consistent with what is understood from His saying: **أَنْ لَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوجاً وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ كافِرُونَ** **"‘The curse of Allah be on the wrongdoers! —those who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked, and who disbelieve in the Hereafter’"** (7:44-45).
